div.div: The workflow
The typical ariel session — from a human request to a verified understanding of what Claude built.
1 of 20 — The workflow — Human kicks things off
The human gives Claude a task. Claude reads the relevant code and begins.
2 of 20 — The workflow — Claude writes code
Claude implements the task. This is fast. The bottleneck is the review that follows.
3 of 20 — The workflow — Loading the DSL
Before writing the walkthrough, Claude runs `ariel guide` to load the full schema into context. This ensures the YAML it writes is valid on the first attempt.
4 of 20 — The workflow — Authoring the walkthrough
Claude authors the walkthrough YAML — identifying decision points, non-obvious design choices, and failure paths worth explaining.
5 of 20 — The workflow — Verify and generate
`ariel verify` catches any errors. `ariel generate` produces a self-contained HTML file.
6 of 20 — The workflow — Handoff
ariel CLI delivers the walkthrough directly to both the human prompter and the reviewer.
7 of 20 — The workflow — Human reviews
The reviewer steps through the walkthrough. One idea per step, one visual per idea. No context-switching.
8 of 20 — The four commands
ariel has four commands. Each exists for a specific moment in the authoring workflow.
9 of 20 — The four commands — guide
guide prints the full DSL reference to stdout — designed to be piped into an LLM's context before authoring a walkthrough file.
10 of 20 — The four commands — verify
verify lints a walkthrough file: YAML syntax, semantic node validation, and Mermaid syntax via an embedded JS runtime. Fast feedback — under one second.
11 of 20 — The four commands — generate
generate produces a self-contained HTML file — all CSS and JS inlined, Mermaid loaded from a pinned CDN. Opens by double-clicking. No server, no dependencies.
12 of 20 — The four commands — watch
watch starts a local HTTP server with live reload. Changes to the YAML push a freshly-rendered page over WebSocket — no manual refresh.
13 of 20 — The pipeline
All three non-guide commands share the same parse pipeline before branching.
14 of 20 — The pipeline — Parsing
The first gate is YAML parsing. Strict mode — unknown fields are errors, not warnings. Failure prints the YAML line number and exits.
15 of 20 — The pipeline — Semantic verification
Semantic verification checks that every node ID referenced in steps actually exists in the mermaid_diagram block.
16 of 20 — The pipeline — Mermaid validation
The third gate validates Mermaid syntax. ariel embeds the full Mermaid JS bundle and runs it via goja — a pure-Go JS runtime. No Node required.
17 of 20 — The pipeline — The router
After all three checks pass, the command branches. verify exits with a success message. generate and watch continue to their output paths.
18 of 20 — The pipeline — generate
generate renders a self-contained HTML file — all CSS and JS inlined, step data baked in as a JS object, Mermaid from a pinned CDN. Double-click to open.
19 of 20 — The pipeline — watch
watch starts an HTTP server serving the same HTML with a WebSocket snippet injected. It opens a browser and watches the file with fsnotify.
20 of 20 — The pipeline — Live reload loop
When the file changes, fsnotify fires and re-runs the full pipeline. The server broadcasts the new HTML over WebSocket and the browser replaces the page.
